title: Luke
content:
آ آ آ آ Wotï؟½s that youï؟½re readinï؟½? a novel? A novel! well, darn my skin!
آ آ آ آ You a man grown and bearded and histinï؟½ such stuff ez that in
آ آ آ آ Stuff about gals and their sweethearts! No wonder youï؟½re thin ez a knife.
آ آ آ آ Look at me clar two hundred and never read one in my life!

آ آ آ آ Thatï؟½s my opinion oï؟½ novels. And ez to their lyinï؟½ round here,
آ آ آ آ They belong to the Jedgeï؟½s daughter the Jedge who came up last year
آ آ آ آ On account of his lungs and the mountains and the balsam oï؟½ pine and fir;
آ آ آ آ And his daughter well, she read novels, and thatï؟½s whatï؟½s the matter with her.

آ آ آ آ Yet she was sweet on the Jedge, and stuck by him day and night,
آ آ آ آ Alone in the cabin up ï؟½yer till she grew like a ghost, all white.
آ آ آ آ She wus only a slip of a thing, ez light and ez up and away
آ آ آ آ Ez rifle smoke blown through the woods, but she wasnï؟½t my kind no way!

آ آ آ آ Speakinï؟½ oï؟½ gals, dï؟½ye mind that house ez you rise the hill,
آ آ آ آ A mile and a half from Whiteï؟½s, and jist above Mattinglyï؟½s mill?
آ آ آ آ You do? Well now tharï؟½s a gal! What! you saw her? Oh, come now, thar! quit!
آ آ آ آ She was only bedevlinï؟½ you boys, for to me she donï؟½t cotton one bit.

آ آ آ آ Now sheï؟½s what I call a gal ez pretty and plump ez a quail;
آ آ آ آ Teeth ez white ez a houndï؟½s, and theyï؟½d go through a ten-penny nail;
آ آ آ آ Eyes that kin snap like a cap. So she asked to know ï؟½whar I was hid?ï؟½
آ آ آ آ She did! Oh, itï؟½s jist like her sass, for sheï؟½s peart ez a Katydid.

آ آ آ آ But what was I talking of? Oh! the Jedge and his daughter she read
آ آ آ آ Novels the whole day long, and I reckon she read them abed;
آ آ آ آ And sometimes she read them out loud to the Jedge on the porch where he sat,
آ آ آ آ And ï؟½twas how ï؟½Lord Augustusï؟½ said this, and how ï؟½Lady Blancheï؟½ she said that.

آ آ آ آ But the sickest of all that I heerd was a yarn thet they read ï؟½bout a chap,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Leather-stockingï؟½ by name, and a hunter chock full oï؟½ the greenest oï؟½ sap;
آ آ آ آ And they asked me to hear, but I says, ï؟½Miss Mabel, not any for me;
آ آ آ آ When I likes I kin sling my own lies, and thet chap and I shouldnï؟½t agree.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ Yet somehow or other that gal allus said that I brought her to mind
آ آ آ آ Of folks about whom she had read, or suthin belike of thet kind,
آ آ آ آ And thar warnï؟½t no end oï؟½ the names that she give me thet summer up here
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Robin Hood,ï؟½ ï؟½Leather-stockingï؟½ ï؟½Rob Roy,ï؟½ Oh, I tell you, the critter was queer!

آ آ آ آ And yet, ef she hadnï؟½t been spiled, she was harmless enough in her way;
آ آ آ آ She could jabber in French to her dad, and they said that she knew how to play;
آ آ آ آ And she worked me that shot-pouch up thar, which the man doesnï؟½t live ez kin use;
آ آ آ آ And slippers you see ï؟½em down ï؟½yer ez would cradle an Injinï؟½s papoose.

آ آ آ آ Yet along oï؟½ them novels, you see, she was wastinï؟½ and mopinï؟½ away,
آ آ آ آ And then she got shy with her tongue, and at last she had nothinï؟½ to say;
آ آ آ آ And whenever I happened around, her face it was hid by a book,
آ آ آ آ And it warnï؟½t till the day she left that she give me ez much ez a look.

آ آ آ آ And this was the way it was. It was night when I kem up here
آ آ آ آ To say to ï؟½em all ï؟½good-by,ï؟½ for I reckoned to go for deer
آ آ آ آ At ï؟½sun upï؟½ the day they left. So I shook ï؟½em all round by the hand,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Cept Mabel, and she was sick, ez they give me to understand.

آ آ آ آ But jist ez I passed the house next morning at dawn, some one,
آ آ آ آ Like a little waver oï؟½ mist got up on the hill with the sun;
آ آ آ آ Miss Mabel it was, alone all wrapped in a mantle oï؟½ lace
آ آ آ آ And she stood there straight in the road, with a touch oï؟½ the sun in her face.

آ آ آ آ And she looked me right in the eye Iï؟½d seen suthinï؟½ like it before
آ آ آ آ When I hunted a wounded doe to the edge oï؟½ the Clear Lake Shore,
آ آ آ آ And I had my knee on its neck, and I jist was raisinï؟½ my knife,
آ آ آ آ When it give me a look like that, and well, it got off with its life.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½We are going to-day,ï؟½ she said, ï؟½and I thought I would say good-by
آ آ آ آ To you in your own house, Luke these woods and the bright blue sky!
آ آ آ آ Youï؟½ve always been kind to us, Luke, and papa has found you still
آ آ آ آ As good as the air he breathes, and wholesome as Laurel Tree Hill.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½And weï؟½ll always think of you, Luke, as the thing we could not take away,
آ آ آ آ The balsam that dwells in the woods, the rainbow that lives in the spray.
آ آ آ آ And youï؟½ll sometimes think of mE, Luke, as you know you once used to say,
آ آ آ آ A rifle smoke blown through the woods, a moment, but never to stay.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ And then we shook hands. She turned, but a-suddent she tottered and fell,
آ آ آ آ And I caught her sharp by the waist, and held her a minit. Well,
آ آ آ آ It was only a minit, you know, thet ez cold and ez white she lay
آ آ آ آ Ez a snowflake here on my breast, and then well, she melted away

آ آ آ آ And was gone. . . . And thar are her books; but I says not any for me;
آ آ آ آ Good enough may be for some, but them and I mightnï؟½t agree.
آ آ آ آ They spiled a decent gal ez might hev made some chap a wife,
آ آ آ آ And look at me! clar two hundred and never read one in my life!
meaning:
This evocative piece by Bret Harte (Francis) represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Bret Harte (Francis)'s bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
